Q1: I tried to set aside time on weekends mostly, since I'm 28 and have other responsibilities during the week. I'd plan to watch the video lectures and take notes, but honestly my routine was pretty inconsistent. Some weeks I'd manage to put in solid hours, other weeks life just got in the way. I'm a visual learner so I really depended on the video content, but I couldn't always stick to a schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd have good intentions at the start of each week, but then work or personal stuff would pile up and I'd end up cramming near deadlines. With 18 courses to juggle, it was really hard to maintain any consistent rhythm. I'd often find myself doing big study sessions right before something was due rather than spreading it out evenly.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or when I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or assignment and that would prompt me to log in. To be honest, I only logged in maybe twice a week on average, which I know wasn't enough. The peer support messages from classmates sometimes reminded me to check in too.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got to the point where logging into the LMS felt automatic or natural. It always felt like something I had to consciously remember to do, and with my low attendance it clearly never became habitual. I think if I had been more consistent early on, maybe it would have stuck, but it just never clicked into my routine.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I spent a lot of time watching them, over three hours total, because I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand. The discussion posts were also helpful when peers explained concepts in different ways. Visual diagrams and any kind of graphic content really helped things stick in my mind.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed the readings and text-heavy materials. They just didn't work as well for my learning style, and when I was already feeling unmotivated, dense text felt overwhelming. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional practice activities because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. The peer interactions were limited too because I felt a bit disconnected from the class.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to the assignments or exams. If I watched a video and immediately understood how it related to what I needed to submit, that felt valuable. When things seemed disconnected or too abstract, I'd lose interest quickly. Also, anything that helped me visualize concepts felt more worthwhile than reading walls of text.
Q8: Yes, optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When I was already behind on required work, doing something optional felt irresponsible somehow. Required activities had that urgency attached to them, even if I didn't always complete them on time. Optional stuff just fell to the bottom of my list and usually stayed there.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download the lecture slides and videos when I could, then study from them on my commute or at home without internet. I also used a notebook where I'd sketch out diagrams and visual notes based on what I'd learned. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the computer.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or looking at printed diagrams for hours, but the LMS wouldn't know anything about it. I'd also sometimes discuss course content with a friend who was taking similar classes, which was really helpful but invisible. My offline review time probably doubled what my online activity showed.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then I'd work with that material for days afterward without going back to the LMS. It was just more convenient to have things saved locally, especially since I wasn't always near reliable internet. So one download might represent several hours of actual study time.
Q12: Around midterm time, things got really hard. I was feeling sad and overwhelmed, and my motivation just tanked. The workload from all 18 courses felt impossible, and I started falling behind on attendance and assignments. That's when the peer support intervention happened, which helped a little, but I never fully recovered my momentum.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big factor since I'm older and have more responsibilities than typical students. When I was exhausted, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from work and life constantly pulled my attention away from the LMS. The confusion from falling behind made it harder to even know where to start.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or materials and my mind would just wander. I'd technically be watching a lecture but thinking about other problems in my life. I felt sad a lot during this course, and that emotional state made it hard to actually engage even when I was physically present with the materials.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. I think part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. The low motivation made everything feel harder than it should have been. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses. It became a cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd ignore things until they were urgent, then scramble to do something. But even then, my assignment completion rate was really low because I'd often miss deadlines entirely or submit incomplete work. The pressure of deadlines stressed me out but didn't always translate into actual productivity.
Q17: When I did get feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly. Positive feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this after all. But I didn't get feedback often enough for it to really shape my behavior, and when feedback was critical, it sometimes made me feel more hopeless. The peer support helped more than instructor feedback in some ways.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across 18 courses. I needed more guidance on what to prioritize. The structure assumed a level of self-direction that I struggled with, especially when my motivation was low. More scaffolding or check-ins might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The visual content made engagement easier for me personally. But the sheer volume of material made it harder, and the lack of clear pathways through the content was confusing. Having so many discussion posts and activities without clear priority made me feel lost. The peer support aspect was helpful but came too late to really turn things around.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying but struggling to understand the material. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, searching for help, but still not able to produce quality work. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism making them afraid to submit. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of struggling, not thriving.
Q21: That makes sense to me because of all the offline studying I did. Someone could download materials once and study extensively without the LMS knowing. Or they might have strong background knowledge and not need as much course interaction. The LMS only sees a slice of what's actually happening in someone's learning process.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logged in and what they clicked on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was actually understanding the video I watched or just letting it play. They miss all the offline work, the emotional struggles, the life circumstances. Logs show behavior but not meaning or depth.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and thinking deeply about the material, not just going through the motions. It means feeling connected to the course and motivated to learn. For me, true engagement would have meant consistent effort and emotional investment, which I struggled to maintain. It's more about mindset than just activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad and unmotivated I felt throughout most of this course. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials or handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the peer conversations that helped me understand concepts, or how much I was juggling outside of school. My engagement was complicated and mostly invisible to the system.
